content.full_text:
State of <California 
< 
Memorandum 
To 
:File 
Date 
November 4, 1977 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Dwight Dickerson 
Subject: Advice Given Over the Phone 
On November 4, 1977, I received a phone call from Palmer 
Madden of the Law Firm of Morrison and Foerster, San Francisco, 
California. 
He described to me the following factual situa-
tion. 
His client sits on a planning commission. 
She also 
has a realty business. 
Her realty firm represents an owner 
who will be affected by a planning commission decision. 
She 
plans not to vote on the issue, but wonders if she may discuss 
the issue with other board members and staff. 
Her activities 
would also include making recommendations on how to vote. 
I told Mr. Madden that his client is precluded not only from 
voting but participating in the decision-making process as 
well. 
This prohibition included all the activities described 
above. 
I referred Mr. Madden to Commission Regulation 18700(c) 
which covers the questioned activities. 
I explained to him 
it was doubtful that she could disassociate herself from 
her office and she should disqualify herself. 
We discussed the possibility that it may be possible for her 
to testify or act on her own behalf when matters related 
solely to her personal interest, but more facts would be 
needed before I could make an actual determination whether 
she could or not. 
DD:ma

sections.question_synthetic: A planning commissioner who owns a realty firm representing an owner affected by a planning commission decision asks whether she may discuss the issue with board members and staff or make voting recommendations, despite planning not to vote herself.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The planning commissioner is precluded from voting and participating in the decision-making process, including discussing the issue with board members, staff, or making recommendations on how to vote. She should disqualify herself, as it is doubtful she can disassociate herself from her office. Limited testimony on matters solely relating to her personal interest may be possible but requires additional facts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an internal FPPC memorandum recording phone advice given. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; advice is embedded in narrative form. Reference to Commission Regulation 18700(c) provided. Caller was attorney Palmer Madden of Morrison and Foerster, not the planning commissioner directly.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A planning commissioner who owns a realty firm representing an owner affected by a planning commission decision asks whether she may discuss the issue with board members and staff or make voting recommendations, despite planning not to vote herself.

CONCLUSION: The planning commissioner is precluded from voting and participating in the decision-making process, including discussing the issue with board members, staff, or making recommendations on how to vote. She should disqualify herself, as it is doubtful she can disassociate herself from her office. Limited testimony on matters solely relating to her personal interest may be possible but requires additional facts.
embedding.first_500_words: State of <California < Memorandum To :File Date November 4, 1977 From FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Dwight Dickerson Subject: Advice Given Over the Phone On November 4, 1977, I received a phone call from Palmer Madden of the Law Firm of Morrison and Foerster, San Francisco, California. He described to me the following factual situa- tion. His client sits on a planning commission. She also has a realty business. Her realty firm represents an owner who will be affected by a planning commission decision. She plans not to vote on the issue, but wonders if she may discuss the issue with other board members and staff. Her activities would also include making recommendations on how to vote. I told Mr. Madden that his client is precluded not only from voting but participating in the decision-making process as well. This prohibition included all the activities described above. I referred Mr. Madden to Commission Regulation 18700(c) which covers the questioned activities. I explained to him it was doubtful that she could disassociate herself from her office and she should disqualify herself. We discussed the possibility that it may be possible for her to testify or act on her own behalf when matters related solely to her personal interest, but more facts would be needed before I could make an actual determination whether she could or not. DD:ma
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum documenting phone advice given to an attorney regarding a planning commissioner's conflict of interest arising from her realty firm's representation of a party affected by a pending commission decision.
